stem:
You are the paediatric registrar at a metropolitan tertiary centre admitting a 13-year-old girl with severe restrictive eating disorder who has not eaten for 10 days and has lost 14 kg over 6 months. She is alert but profoundly fatigued. She weighs 32 kg with a height of 158 cm (BMI 12.8 kg/m2). She has bradycardia and hypothermia. The dietitian asks for your prescription before commencing feeding via nasogastric tube.

The team plans to use a polymeric formula at 20 kcal/kg/day initially. Her admission electrolytes are at the lower end of normal but no oral or intravenous supplementation has yet been given.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 38 bpm, BP 88/52 mmHg, Temp 35.4 degrees C, RR 14, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Severe wasting, lanugo, cold peripheries, normal capillary refill 2 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Phosphate 0.84 mmol/L, magnesium 0.72 mmol/L, potassium 3.7 mmol/L, glucose 3.8 mmol/L, ALT 92 U/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention before commencing enteral feeds?

options.text: Withhold all feeds for 48 hours while correcting electrolytes intravenously and observing on a cardiac monitor
options.rationale: Prolonged starvation worsens cardiac and metabolic instability. Cautious feeding with concurrent prophylaxis is preferred over further fasting, which increases mortality in this cohort. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Refeeding Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous thiamine 100 mg followed by oral phosphate and magnesium supplementation, then start feeds
options.rationale: In severe malnutrition with prolonged starvation, prophylactic thiamine before any carbohydrate load prevents Wernicke encephalopathy, and pre-emptive electrolyte replacement covers the anticipated refeeding shifts even when baseline levels are within normal range. Australian eating disorder guidelines recommend this prophylactic bundle for high-risk refeeding before commencing nutrition. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Refeeding Guidelines)

options.text: Commence enteral feeds at 40 kcal/kg/day immediately to address weight loss and reassess electrolytes daily
options.rationale: Starting at 40 kcal/kg/day in a severely malnourished adolescent without prophylaxis precipitates classical refeeding syndrome with phosphate, magnesium, and potassium crashes. Australian guidelines advise starting low and advancing cautiously with prophylactic micronutrients. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Refeeding Guidelines)

options.text: Give a single intravenous bolus of dextrose 10% 5 mL/kg to raise glucose before nasogastric feeding commences
options.rationale: A rapid carbohydrate load in a thiamine-deplete malnourished patient is a recognised trigger for both Wernicke encephalopathy and acute refeeding syndrome. Thiamine and electrolyte cover must precede any carbohydrate. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Refeeding Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous parenteral nutrition rather than enteral feeds to reduce the refeeding metabolic shift
options.rationale: Parenteral nutrition does not lower refeeding risk and carries additional line and metabolic complications. Cautious enteral nutrition with prophylactic thiamine and electrolytes is the Australian standard. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Refeeding Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome arises from the abrupt intracellular shift of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium driven by insulin release when carbohydrate is reintroduced after prolonged starvation. Adolescents with severe restrictive eating disorders and weight loss exceeding 15% are at highest risk. Prophylaxis with thiamine 100 to 300 mg daily and replacement of phosphate, magnesium, and potassium before and during early feeding prevents the cardiac, neurological, and respiratory complications. Initial feed volume is started low (around 20 kcal/kg/day) and titrated up over 7 to 10 days with daily electrolyte checks.
key_points: Refeeding risk highest with weight loss above 15%, prolonged starvation, or BMI under 14
Thiamine 100 to 300 mg daily before any carbohydrate load prevents Wernicke encephalopathy
Phosphate, magnesium, and potassium replaced prophylactically even when baseline levels appear normal
Initial feed at around 20 kcal/kg/day advancing over 7 to 10 days with daily electrolytes
explanation.pearls: Thiamine must precede any glucose or feed in severe malnutrition: a single intravenous bolus of dextrose into a thiamine-deplete adolescent can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy within hours.

sources.label: NICE NG69 - Eating Disorders Recognition and Treatment

stem: You are the paediatric registrar called urgently to the special care nursery. A 3-day-old term male infant has become progressively lethargic with poor feeding over 18 hours and now has tachypnoea without respiratory distress and intermittent posturing. He was born at 39 weeks after an uncomplicated pregnancy. He fed eagerly for the first 36 hours then deteriorated. Birth weight was 3380 g. A septic screen has been sent and cultures are pending. The mother had a sister who died in the neonatal period of unknown cause.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR 78, Temp 36.6 degrees C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Lethargic, intermittent extensor posturing, mild hepatomegaly, anterior fontanelle full
data_table.Investigations: pH 7.49, pCO2 22 mmHg, bicarbonate 16 mmol/L, lactate 1.8 mmol/L, glucose 3.6 mmol/L, ammonia 420 micromol/L, ALT 38 U/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Stop protein-containing feeds, commence intravenous dextrose with intralipid, and arrange urgent haemodialysis
options.rationale: Plasma ammonia above 400 micromol/L in a neonate with encephalopathy is a metabolic emergency consistent with a urea cycle disorder or organic acidaemia, with respiratory alkalosis driven by central ammonia toxicity. Immediate protein withdrawal, anabolic dextrose support, and dialysis are required to halt neurotoxicity. Family history of neonatal death increases the prior probability of an inborn error. (Source: HGSA - Metabolic Emergency Guidelines)

options.text: Increase enteral feeds with a high-protein neonatal formula to support growth and metabolic recovery
options.rationale: Protein loading in a neonate with hyperammonaemia accelerates ammonia generation and worsens encephalopathy. Protein must be withdrawn for 24 to 48 hours while ammonia falls. (Source: HGSA - Metabolic Emergency Guidelines)

options.text: Commence intravenous aciclovir for presumed neonatal herpes encephalitis and await CSF results
options.rationale: Aciclovir is appropriate empirically when HSV encephalitis is suspected, but the combination of normal CRP, profound hyperammonaemia, respiratory alkalosis, and family history strongly indicates an inborn error. Treatment of the metabolic emergency cannot be deferred for an HSV workup. (Source: HGSA - Metabolic Emergency Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg to correct acidosis and continue current feeds
options.rationale: The blood gas shows respiratory alkalosis, not acidosis - bicarbonate is contraindicated and worsens the alkalaemia. The therapeutic priority is ammonia removal and anabolic support. (Source: HGSA - Metabolic Emergency Guidelines)

options.text: Commence phenobarbitone for presumed neonatal seizures and observe in the nursery overnight
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone treats clinical seizures but does not address the underlying hyperammonaemia, which is the primary driver of neurological injury. Without ammonia clearance, irreversible neurotoxicity progresses within hours. (Source: HGSA - Metabolic Emergency Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Neonatal hyperammonaemia is a metabolic emergency typically due to urea cycle disorders or organic acidaemias. The classical pattern is a previously well term neonate who deteriorates 24 to 72 hours after feeding establishes, with lethargy, tachypnoea, and respiratory alkalosis from central ammonia stimulation. Plasma ammonia above 200 micromol/L in a sick neonate prompts urgent metabolic workup and treatment, and above 400 micromol/L triggers preparation for renal replacement therapy. The therapeutic bundle is stop protein, give anabolic dextrose with intralipid, give scavenger drugs (sodium benzoate, sodium phenylbutyrate) where available, and dialyse.
key_points: Urea cycle disorders present with respiratory alkalosis; organic acidaemias with metabolic acidosis and anion gap
Ammonia above 400 micromol/L mandates urgent haemodialysis
Protein must be stopped within 24 to 48 hours; anabolic IV dextrose plus intralipid supports caloric needs
Family history of unexplained neonatal death is a strong clue for inherited metabolic disease
explanation.pearls: A previously well term neonate who deteriorates after 36 hours of successful feeding with respiratory alkalosis and ammonia above 200 micromol/L should be assumed to have a urea cycle disorder until proven otherwise, with dialysis prep at ammonia 400 micromol/L.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Suspected Inborn Error of Metabolism

stem:
You are the emergency registrar at a regional Queensland hospital during a 41 degrees C heatwave. A 15-year-old boy is brought in by ambulance after collapsing 90 minutes into a school cross-country event. He has been confused since collapse. He is now obtunded with hot dry skin despite cold packs applied in transit. Bystander first-aid included spraying him with water and fanning.

He has no known medical conditions. Weight is 60 kg. Estimated time since collapse is 35 minutes. Core (rectal) temperature has been recorded as 41.6 degrees C.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 152 bpm, BP 86/50 mmHg, RR 32, Temp 41.6 degrees C rectal, SpO2 95% room air
data_table.Examination: GCS 9 (E2 V3 M4), hot skin, capillary refill 4 seconds, no neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 5.4 mmol/L, lactate 6.8 mmol/L, potassium 5.6 mmol/L, creatinine 158 micromol/L, CK 12500 U/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate cooling strategy?

options.text: Iced gastric lavage via nasogastric tube as the primary cooling strategy in the resuscitation cubicle
options.rationale: Gastric lavage achieves much slower cooling than immersion and carries aspiration risk in an obtunded patient. It is at best an adjunct, never a primary strategy. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.4 - Heat-Induced Illness)

options.text: Tepid sponging with continuous fanning while administering intravenous paracetamol 1 g and dantrolene
options.rationale: Tepid sponging cools too slowly to prevent multi-organ injury in exertional heatstroke. Antipyretics do not work in heatstroke because the hypothalamic set-point is normal, and dantrolene has no proven role outside malignant hyperthermia. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.4 - Heat-Induced Illness)

options.text: Cold-water immersion to bring core temperature to 39 degrees C as rapidly as possible while supporting airway and circulation
options.rationale: In exertional heatstroke, every minute above 40 degrees C drives multi-organ injury, and cold-water immersion is the most rapid cooling method, lowering core temperature by approximately 0.2 degrees C per minute. Cooling targets cessation at 39 degrees C to avoid rebound hypothermia. Airway and circulation are supported in parallel rather than sequentially. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.4 - Heat-Induced Illness)

options.text: Cooled intravenous saline 30 mL/kg over 30 minutes as the primary cooling method with target normothermia
options.rationale: Cooled IV fluids contribute only modest cooling rates compared with immersion. Aggressive fluid loading also risks pulmonary oedema in an adolescent with normal cardiac function and threatens cooling target overshoot. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.4 - Heat-Induced Illness)

options.text: Defer aggressive cooling until intubation is performed and central venous access is established
options.rationale: Cooling must commence immediately; mortality rises with every minute above 40 degrees C. Intubation and central access proceed in parallel with cold-water immersion, not before it. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 9.3.4 - Heat-Induced Illness)

explanation.summary: Exertional heatstroke is defined by core temperature above 40 degrees C with central nervous system dysfunction occurring during physical exertion. Mortality rises sharply for every minute spent above 40 degrees C, so the maxim is cool first, transport second. Cold-water immersion is the gold standard with cooling rates of approximately 0.2 degrees C per minute. Adjunct measures (cooled IV fluids, evaporative cooling, ice packs to groin and axillae) are added if immersion is not immediately available. Complications include rhabdomyolysis, AKI, DIC, hepatic failure, and ARDS.
key_points: Exertional heatstroke: core above 40 degrees C plus CNS dysfunction during exertion
Cold-water immersion cools at around 0.2 degrees C per minute, faster than any other method
Stop active cooling at 39 degrees C to avoid rebound hypothermia
Rhabdomyolysis, AKI and DIC are predictable complications and require admission to a high-acuity unit
explanation.pearls: Antipyretics like paracetamol do not work in heatstroke because the hypothalamic set-point is normal and the elevated core temperature is from environmental load, not endogenous fever - the only effective treatment is rapid external cooling, ideally cold-water immersion.

sources.label: Sports Medicine Australia - Hot Weather Guidelines

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar reviewing a woman at 38+5 weeks of her second pregnancy in early labour with regular contractions every 4 minutes. Membranes ruptured 90 minutes ago with clear liquor. Her routine 36-week vagino-rectal swab was positive for group B streptococcus. She has no penicillin allergy. Her temperature is normal and the cardiotocograph is reassuring. Her first child was healthy with no neonatal GBS disease. The cervix is 5 cm dilated. The midwife asks for your antibiotic prescription.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88 bpm, BP 122/76 mmHg, Temp 36.8 degrees C, RR 16
data_table.Examination: Cervix 5 cm, station -1, vertex, clear liquor, CTG reactive baseline 130 bpm
data_table.Investigations: 36-week vagino-rectal swab: GBS positive; FBC normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Ampicillin 2 g intravenously now then 1 g every 4 hours until birth without further dosing
options.rationale: Although ampicillin is acceptable when benzylpenicillin is unavailable, it is broader-spectrum and increases neonatal resistant Gram-negative colonisation risk. Benzylpenicillin remains first-line in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Benzylpenicillin 3 g intravenously now then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g 4-hourly is the recommended Australian intrapartum GBS prophylaxis regimen for women without penicillin allergy. Adequate prophylaxis requires at least 4 hours before delivery to achieve neonatal protection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Cefazolin 2 g intravenously now then 1 g every 8 hours until birth as a beta-lactam alternative
options.rationale: Cefazolin is reserved for women with a non-severe penicillin allergy. In a woman with no allergy, benzylpenicillin is preferred for its narrower spectrum. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Clindamycin 600 mg intravenously now then 6-hourly until birth as an alternative regimen
options.rationale: Clindamycin is used only when there is severe penicillin allergy and confirmed clindamycin susceptibility on the antenatal swab. It is not first-line and rates of resistance approach 20%. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

options.text: Amoxicillin 1 g orally now and continue every 8 hours until birth as an oral prophylactic option
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics do not provide reliable intrapartum prophylaxis because of variable absorption and slower achievement of bactericidal cord blood levels. Intravenous beta-lactam is the only acceptable route. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus

explanation.summary: Australian intrapartum GBS prophylaxis screens women with a routine vagino-rectal swab at 35 to 37 weeks, with risk-based prophylaxis applied if screening has not occurred. Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.8 g 4-hourly is first-line. Cefazolin is used for non-severe penicillin allergy; clindamycin or lincomycin with confirmed susceptibility for severe allergy; vancomycin if susceptibility unknown. Adequate prophylaxis is defined as at least one dose given at least 4 hours before delivery, and neonatal management is informed by this timing.
key_points: Benzylpenicillin 3 g then 1.8 g 4-hourly is first-line intrapartum prophylaxis in Australia
Cefazolin for non-severe penicillin allergy; clindamycin requires confirmed susceptibility
Adequate prophylaxis is defined as at least 4 hours before delivery
Risk-based prophylaxis used when screening unknown (preterm labour, prolonged ROM, fever, prior affected infant)
explanation.pearls: Adequate GBS prophylaxis is defined as the first dose given at least 4 hours before delivery: babies whose mothers received only 1 to 3 hours of antibiotics still need clinical observation for 24 to 48 hours.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Group B Streptococcus
RANZCOG - Screening and Treatment for Group B Streptococcus
CDC - Prevention of Group B Streptococcal Disease

stem: You are a general practice registrar reviewing a 32-year-old woman attending for routine preventive care. Her last cervical screening test 5 years ago detected oncogenic HPV (non-16/18) with reflex cytology showing low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion. She subsequently had a 12-month repeat test which was HPV negative. She has been in a monogamous relationship for 4 years, is up to date with the human papillomavirus vaccination, has had no abnormal bleeding, and is asymptomatic. She is requesting advice about further screening.

data_table.Examination: BMI 24 kg/m2, abdomen unremarkable, no pelvic complaints
data_table.Investigations: Five-year prior CST: HPV (non-16/18) positive, LSIL on reflex LBC; 4-year prior CST (repeat at 12 months): HPV negative

lead_in: When should her next cervical screening test be performed?

options.text: Now as a routine cervical screening test, returning her to the standard 5-yearly screening interval
options.rationale: After return to negative on the 12-month repeat following non-16/18 HPV with LSIL reflex cytology, the woman returns to routine 5-yearly screening from the date of that clear test. Her last test was 4 years ago so she is due her next routine CST now. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

options.text: In 1 year as a further surveillance test before returning to routine 5-yearly screening
options.rationale: Only a single 12-month surveillance test is recommended after intermediate-risk results when the surveillance test is negative. Repeat 12-month testing is not required after a clear surveillance test. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

options.text: In 2 years to align with the previous biennial Pap smear schedule and confirm sustained clearance
options.rationale: Australia transitioned from 2-yearly cytology in 2017 to 5-yearly HPV-based screening for women aged 25 to 74. Biennial scheduling no longer applies. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to colposcopy now for direct visualisation given her prior LSIL on reflex cytology
options.rationale: Colposcopy is not indicated after low-risk return to negative. LSIL with non-16/18 HPV requires only the 12-month surveillance, which she has already completed normally. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

options.text: Not require further screening as she has been vaccinated against human papillomavirus
options.rationale: HPV vaccination is highly effective but does not cover all oncogenic types, and vaccinated women remain in the National Cervical Screening Program at the same intervals. (Source: NCSP Clinical Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The renewed National Cervical Screening Program uses 5-yearly primary HPV testing for women aged 25 to 74. Detected non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with low-grade reflex cytology is managed as intermediate risk and triggers a 12-month repeat HPV test, after which a clear result returns the woman to routine 5-yearly screening from that date. HPV 16 or 18 detection at any cytology triggers direct colposcopy referral. Vaccinated women remain in the program because vaccination does not cover all oncogenic types.
key_points: Routine cervical screening: 5-yearly HPV-based testing for women 25 to 74
Non-16/18 HPV with negative or low-grade cytology: 12-month repeat HPV
HPV 16 or 18 detection at any cytology: direct colposcopy
Self-collected vaginal swabs are now an equivalent option for primary HPV testing
explanation.pearls: After a clear 12-month surveillance HPV test following an intermediate-risk result, the woman returns to routine 5-yearly screening dated from that surveillance test - not from the original abnormal test.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia - Cervical Cancer Screening
